summary: Zhuge Liang; the famous arrow-borrowing chapter. Boasts to Zhou Yu he can produce 100,000 arrows in three days; sets out at night in mist with twenty straw-dummy boats; arrives at Cao Cao's anchorage with drums beating; Cao Cao's archers shoot blindly until both sides of every dummy bristle with arrows. Returns with the full 100,000. Zhou Yu sighs 'I am not his match'.

Zhou Yu; sets Zhuge Liang the 100,000-arrow trap as a pretext for execution; defeated when Zhuge Liang produces them in three days. Then approves Huang Gai's flesh-suffering plan — has Huang Gai beaten publicly until his back is bloody so Cao Cao will believe his coming defection.

Huang Gai; volunteers to undergo a brutal public beating so Cao Cao will believe his subsequent defection — the 'flesh-suffering' scheme. Lies bleeding in his tent at chapter's end; Kan Ze visits to deliver the false-surrender letter to Cao Cao.
Huang Gai (duplicate record).
Cao Cao; orders the arrow-volley on the misty river at Zhou Yu's dummies — gives Zhuge Liang the 100,000 he needed. Will fall for Huang Gai's defection letter in the next chapter.
Lu Su; accompanies Zhuge Liang on the misty arrow-borrowing run and is terrified throughout.
Gan Ning; with Zhou Yu at the war council.
Jiang Gan; named in passing — Zhou Yu plans to send him on a second errand in the next chapter.
Kan Ze; the rare scholar in Sun Quan's circle willing to risk himself. Sees through Huang Gai's beating immediately; volunteers to carry the false-surrender letter to Cao Cao.
Mao Jie; serves as nominal admiral on Cao Cao's side.
Yu Jin; co-admiral with Mao Jie on Cao Cao's side.
Cai Mao; named in passing — already executed.
Sun Jian; named in passing as Sun Quan's late father.
Xun You; with Cao Cao.
Zhang Zhao; civil officer at Sun Quan's court.
Zhang Zhao (duplicate record).

Kan Ze; carries Huang Gai's false-surrender letter to Cao Cao's camp at night in fisherman's disguise. Cao Cao reads the letter for treachery; Kan Ze improvises through a hostile interrogation with such bravado that Cao Cao believes him. The chapter's standout cool-under-pressure scene.

Pang Tong; arrives at Cao Cao's camp under the guise of a Daoist scholar visiting his old friend; sells Cao Cao the chain-the-ships scheme — link the boats together with iron chains so the seasick northern soldiers will sail steadier. Cao Cao adopts it instantly. The Red Cliffs fire trap is now possible.
Cao Cao; accepts Huang Gai's false-surrender letter; orders the ships chained on Pang Tong's recommendation. Both moves are death sentences he will only realise too late.

Jiang Gan; sent back to Wu by Cao Cao with a second mission; this time Zhou Yu sends Pang Tong out to meet him 'by accident', knowing Jiang Gan will recommend him to Cao Cao. The trap that delivers Pang Tong to the right camp.
Zhou Yu; uses Jiang Gan a second time — this time to insert Pang Tong into Cao Cao's camp. The double application of the same gullible envoy.
Huang Gai; lying in his tent recovering from the beating; waits for the signal to launch the fire ships.
Huang Gai (duplicate record).
Gan Ning; agrees with Kan Ze to receive Cai Mao's two nephews (the remaining Cai officers) as inside agents — Wu doubles down on the misinformation.
Lu Su; with Zhou Yu.
Sun Quan; off-stage at Chaisang.
Liu Bei; off-stage at Jiangxia.
Sun Zi; named in passing in a rhetorical aside about the art of war.
Wu Qi; named in passing alongside Sun Zi as classical military reference.

Cao Cao; the Short Song chapter. Holds the great banquet on the river under the moon; composes the famous 'Short Song' poem ('the moon is bright, stars are few...'). Liu Fu interrupts to criticise a verse as inauspicious; Cao Cao spears him with a long-handled spear. Mortified the next morning but does not recover. First skirmish goes against him; the flag falls. The omens of doom build.
Liu Fu; interrupts Cao Cao's poem to question its inauspicious lines; Cao Cao kills him with the long-handled spear at the table. Cao Cao's first major remorse the next morning.
Liu Fu (duplicate record).
Liu Fu (duplicate record).

Pang Tong; takes his leave from Cao Cao's camp; meets Xu Shu on the way out — Xu Shu sees through the chained-ships plan but Pang Tong gives him an escape route (asks for the Western expedition to remove him from the Red Cliffs disaster).
Xu Shu; sees Pang Tong's trap immediately. Takes Pang Tong's advice to request a posting against Ma Teng / Han Sui in the west — saving himself from the Red Cliffs fire.
Ma Teng; named in passing — the threat Xu Shu uses as a pretext to leave Cao Cao's army.
Han Sui; named in passing alongside Ma Teng.

Zhou Yu; observes the river fleet from the southern bank; sees Cao Cao's flag fall in the wind. Suddenly seizes his chest, coughs blood, and falls — wounded by his realisation that the southeast wind he needs for the fire attack is the wrong season's wind. The setup for Zhuge Liang's wind-calling ritual.
Zhuge Liang; off-stage in the chapter; called for at chapter's end by the prostrate Zhou Yu — only Zhuge Liang knows what's wrong.
Lu Su; with Zhou Yu when he collapses.
Li Tong; with Cao Cao at the banquet.
Li Tong (duplicate record).
Cao Hong; with Cao Cao.
Cao Hong (duplicate record).
Xiahou Dun; with Cao Cao.
Xiahou Yuan; with Cao Cao.
Xu Chu; with Cao Cao.
Xu Huang; with Cao Cao.
Yu Jin; co-admiral; commands part of the first river skirmish.
Mao Jie; co-admiral; commands part of the first river skirmish.
Zhang He; with Cao Cao.
Zhang Liao; with Cao Cao.
Cheng Yu; counsels Cao Cao at the river — warns that the chained ships are vulnerable to fire. Cao Cao laughs it off citing the wind direction.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Xun You; with Cao Cao.
Han Dang; commands part of Zhou Yu's first naval skirmish.
Zhou Tai; commands part of Zhou Yu's first naval skirmish.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).
Wen Pin; commands part of Cao Cao's first river skirmish.
Li Dian; with Cao Cao.
Liu Bei; off-stage at Jiangxia.
Elder Qiao; named in passing — the Qiao sisters are still nominally the trigger Zhou Yu thinks about.
Sun Ce; named in passing as Da Qiao's husband.
Liu Xi; minor figure named in passing.
Liu Xi (duplicate record).
Liu Xi (duplicate record).
Liu Xi (duplicate record).
Jiao Chu; minor figure named in passing.
Liang Da; minor figure named in passing.
Yuan Shao; named in passing.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
Yuan Shu; named in passing.
Zang Ba; with Cao Cao.

Zhuge Liang; sets up the seven-star altar on Nanping Hill and 'borrows' the southeast wind through three nights of ritual. Sneaks off in Zhao Yun's boat the moment the wind rises — knowing Zhou Yu will try to kill him as soon as the campaign succeeds.
Zhou Yu; orders Ding Feng and Xu Sheng to kill Zhuge Liang the moment the southeast wind has done its work. Misses by minutes. Then launches the fire ships under Huang Gai's command.

Huang Gai; leads the fire-ships into Cao Cao's chained fleet at the third watch under the southeast wind. Twenty vessels stuffed with pitch and oil ignite the entire Cao Cao navy. The defining stratagem of Red Cliffs.
Huang Gai (duplicate record).

Cao Cao; the Red Cliffs disaster. Sees Huang Gai's flotilla approach with the false-surrender flag, realises only at the last moment what's coming, scrambles for the shore as fire envelops the chained ships. Loses two-thirds of his army in a single night.
Kan Ze; with Huang Gai on the fire-ships.
Ding Feng; rides with Xu Sheng to assassinate Zhuge Liang at Nanping Hill — arrives just after Zhuge Liang has slipped away in Zhao Yun's boat.
Ding Feng (duplicate record).
Xu Sheng; rides with Ding Feng on the failed assassination of Zhuge Liang.
Zhao Yun; receives Zhuge Liang's secret pre-arranged signal and slips Zhuge Liang out of the altar in his boat just before Ding Feng arrives.
Liu Bei; receives Zhuge Liang's secret deployment orders for the post-Red Cliffs scramble for Jingzhou's cities. Begins positioning his army for the empty grab while Zhou Yu and Cao Cao slug it out.
Guan Yu; given Huarong Valley as his ambush position. Liu Bei warns that Guan Yu's old gratitude to Cao Cao may make him soft — but Guan Yu signs a written pledge of execution to take the post.
Zhang Fei; given the Yiling road ambush; will kill any of Cao Cao's officers who try that route.
Guan Ping; with Guan Yu at the Huarong Valley ambush.
Liu Feng; commands one of Liu Bei's ambush detachments.
Cheng Pu; commands part of Zhou Yu's army during the fire attack.
Gan Ning; commands part of Zhou Yu's army; takes the lead in pursuing Cao Cao's fleeing remnants.
Han Dang; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Jiang Qin; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Pan Zhang; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Chen Wu; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Dong Xi; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Zhou Tai; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Zhou Tai (duplicate record).
Taishi Ci; commands part of Zhou Yu's army at Hefei.
Lu Xun; commands part of Zhou Yu's army.
Lu Su; with Zhou Yu at the post-battle council.
Sun Quan; with the Wu reserves.
Zhang Liao; with Cao Cao in the burning fleet — rescues Cao Cao from the flames in a small boat.
Cheng Yu; with Cao Cao in the burning fleet.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Wen Pin; commands part of Cao Cao's fleet — caught in the fire.
Liu Qi; at Jiangxia.
Jian Yong; with Liu Bei.
Mi Fang; with Liu Bei.
Mi Zhu; with Liu Bei.
Sun Qian; with Liu Bei.
Sun Qian (duplicate record).
Yan Liang; named in passing — long dead.
Wen Chou; named in passing — long dead.
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.
Liang Da; minor figure named in passing.

Cao Cao; the Huarong Valley flight — three nights through swamps and burning hills with his army shrinking by the hour. Laughs three times at supposed gaps in his enemies' plans; each laugh draws a fresh ambush (Zhao Yun, Zhang Fei, then finally Guan Yu). At Huarong Valley, Guan Yu lets him pass in repayment of the old debt. Reaches Nanjun with only 27 survivors of the great fleet.

Guan Yu at Huarong Valley — Cao Cao falls to his knees and recalls every kindness from chapter 25 onward; Guan Yu remembers the gifts, the seal he refused, the ride past five passes Cao Cao didn't avenge. Steps aside, breaking his written pledge — and accepting Liu Bei's mercy at chapter's end.
Zhao Yun; ambushes Cao Cao's first laugh; routs the fleeing column at the first turn.
Zhang Fei; ambushes Cao Cao's second laugh at the Hulu Valley; sends Xu Chu and Zhang Liao fleeing.

Zhuge Liang; receives Guan Yu's return at chapter's end. Pretends to order Guan Yu's execution for the broken pledge; reverses on Liu Bei's intercession. The dramatic device that returns the brothers to balance.
Cao Hong; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Cao Hong (duplicate record).
Cao Ren; assembles the survivors at Nanjun and prepares the city for the inevitable Wu attack.
Xiahou Dun; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Xu Chu; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Xu Huang; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Zhang He; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Zhang Liao; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Li Dian; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Cheng Yu; with Cao Cao in the Huarong flight.
Cheng Yu (duplicate record).
Guo Jia; named in passing — Cao Cao laments 'if Guo Jia had lived, I would not be in this position'.
Mao Jie; named in passing — with Cao Cao's remnant fleet.
Kuai Yue; with Cao Cao.
Wen Pin; with Cao Cao's burning fleet.
Huang Gai; wounded by an arrow during the pursuit; pulled from the water by Han Dang.
Huang Gai (duplicate record).
Han Dang; rescues Huang Gai from the water during the pursuit.
Gan Ning; commands the Wu pursuit.
Zhou Yu; commands the Wu pursuit; ends the chapter chasing toward Nanjun for the next-chapter race with Liu Bei.
Lu Xun; commands part of the Wu pursuit.
Taishi Ci; commands part of the Wu army.
Sun Quan; with the Wu reserves.
Ma Yan; minor figure named in passing.
Zhang Zi; minor figure named in passing.
Shentu Pan; minor figure named in passing.
Yuan Shao; named in passing — Cao Cao thinks of how the north was harder to win than this loss is to bear.
Yuan Shao (duplicate record).
